claims.text: On 25 June 2026 a magnitude 7.5 earthquake struck 16 km SW of Moron in northern Venezuela, west of Caracas.

OCHA's Situation Report #22 (15 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that authorities report a toll of 4,829 people dead, 16,740 injured and 6,462 rescued since the 24 June earthquakes; 17,907 people remain without housing.

OCHA's Situation Report #23 (16 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that authorities report a toll of 4,930 people dead, 16,740 injured and 6,462 rescued since the 24 June earthquakes, with more than 1,308 aftershocks recorded and 17,907 people remaining without housing. The additional appeal was raised to US$299 million, still targeting 1.3 million people over six months. EU Commissioner for Crisis Management Hadja Lahbib conducted a field visit to La Guaira together with the UN Humanitarian Coordinator to review response efforts.

OCHA's Situation Report #24 (17 July 2026, 8:00 pm) states that, twenty-three days since the earthquakes, authorities report an official toll of 5,069 people dead and 16,740 injured, with 1,331 aftershocks recorded since the 24 June earthquakes (magnitude 7.2 and 7.5). An additional appeal for US$299 million remains in place to scale up the earthquake response for 1.3 million people over the next six months.
The USGS is assessing earthquake-triggered landslide hazards generated by the 2026 Venezuela earthquake sequence.

As of 1 July 2026, nearly 2,000 deaths have been confirmed and more than 6,400 people rescued; 1.8 million people are in need of humanitarian assistance including 680,000 children across six affected states, with around 1,000 buildings and over 400 schools damaged and more than 600 aftershocks recorded since the initial quakes.

OCHA's Situation Report #14 (07 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that authorities report an official toll of 3,685 people dead and 16,740 injured, with 6,462 people rescued since the 24 June earthquakes. Authorities also reported that approximately 17,907 people lost their homes and that more than 1,076 aftershocks have been recorded, with at least seven (7) states impacted and La Guaira remaining the most affected state.

OCHA's Situation Report #15 (08 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that authorities report an official toll of 3,811 people dead and 16,740 injured, with 6,462 people rescued since the 24 June earthquakes; approximately 17,907 people lost their homes and more than 1,100 aftershocks have been recorded. An additional appeal for US$296 million was launched to scale up the earthquake response for 1.3 million people over the next six months.

A first assessment by the UN Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR) estimates direct physical damage to housing and infrastructure at some $37 billion, comprising $24 billion in damage to buildings and another $13 billion in infrastructure; at least 79 transitional camps have opened in stadiums and sports centres, hosting displaced residents and providing aid.

OCHA's Situation Report #18 (11 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that seventeen days after the earthquakes, authorities report a toll of 4,333 people dead, 16,740 injured and 6,462 rescued since the onset of the emergency; 1,171 aftershocks have been recorded, 17,907 people remain without housing, and 315 of those who died have yet to be identified. UN humanitarian chief Tom Fletcher concluded his four-day visit to the country and called for sustained international support for the Humanitarian Response Plan; an additional appeal for US$298 million was launched to address the multisectoral needs of 1.3 million people over the next six months.

OCHA's Situation Report No. 20 (13 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that, nineteen days since the earthquakes, authorities report a toll of 4,561 people dead, 16,740 injured and 6,462 rescued since the onset of the emergency, with more than 1,254 aftershocks recorded and 17,907 people remaining without housing. Authorities report at least 856 affected buildings, including 190 that have collapsed. The response continues with 107 transitional camps supporting affected populations, with a capacity of 25,064 people and a current occupancy of 20,231 people. A preliminary UNDP estimate indicates direct physical damage of US$6.7 billion, equivalent to 6 percent of GDP, alongside the earlier UNDRR estimate of approximately US$37 billion. The Government of Venezuela announced the creation of an initial reconstruction fund equivalent to US$200 million.

OCHA's Situation Report #21 (14 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that authorities report a toll of 4,734 people dead, 16,740 injured and 6,462 rescued since the 24 June earthquakes; 17,907 people remain without housing.

description: A magnitude 7.5 earthquake struck northern Venezuela west of Caracas on 25 June 2026, 39 seconds after a magnitude 7.2 foreshock in the same sequence. The USGS assessed that high casualties and widespread damage are probable, and the national government of Venezuela declared a state of emergency. Twenty-three days on, the confirmed death toll has continued to climb past 5,000, and the UN has assessed billions of dollars in physical damage.

impact.summary: As of OCHA's Situation Report #24 (17 July 2026, 8:00 pm, published 18 July), authorities report an official toll of 5,069 people dead and 16,740 injured; 1,331 aftershocks have been recorded since the 24 June earthquakes. An additional appeal of US$299 million targets 1.3 million people over six months, on top of the US$632 million 2026 Humanitarian Response Plan covering 5.5 million people. Earlier assessments put direct physical damage at $37 billion (UNDRR) and $6.7 billion / 6% of GDP (UNDP).

location.region: Near Morón / San Felipe, northern Venezuela (west of Caracas)

name: Venezuela M7.5 Earthquake Doublet
